fingerprints: Next.js App Router is in use rather than a Pages Router-only deployment
GET /{app_route}?_rsc={cache_buster} with RSC headers returns text/x-component or another React Flight response
POST Server Action fetches are handled on page routes via the next-action header
signals: Responses show text/x-component, _rsc cache-busting behavior, or x-nextjs-* headers associated with App Router traffic
Malformed or invalid Server Action probes trigger Next.js-specific action parsing errors or x-nextjs-action-not-found
The deployment has not yet been upgraded to 15.0.5 / 15.1.9 / 15.2.6 / 15.3.6 / 15.4.8 / 15.5.7 / 16.0.7 or later

preconditions: The target must run a vulnerable Next.js release that uses the App Router and affected React Server Components packages.
Attacker-controlled requests must reach the Node-side RSC / Server Action handling path without an upstream proxy stripping Next.js-specific headers.
At minimum a reachable RSC surface must exist; some exploit paths additionally require a callable Server Function or Action surface on the chosen route.
Do not rely on hosting-provider WAF behavior as proof of safety; official advisories require patching even when temporary mitigations are present.

matchers: Response body resembles React Flight / RSC frames or a digest-style parser error
Redirects or rewrites still preserve App Router / _rsc behavior
x-nextjs-action-not-found: 1 or another Next.js Server Action handling branch is triggered
404 or 500 responses still reference action parsing rather than a generic route handler
Substitute {cmd} only after the RSC surface and action parsing path are confirmed in an authorized environment
For low-noise validation, start with a harmless command placeholder rather than a long post-exploitation sequence

stabilization: Keep the first proof minimal: verify execution context with whoami, hostname, pwd, node -v, and only then inspect application internals.
Prioritize .next/server artifacts, server-reference manifests, route manifests, and deployment metadata before attempting noisier actions.
If the target was exposed before patching, assume secrets may already be at risk; any defensive remediation workflow should include credential and token rotation.

shell_options.template: Reuse the validated request shape and replace {cmd} with the single authorized command you want to test in your lab environment.
Embed a low-noise runtime probe such as `node -v && whoami && pwd && ls -la .next` in the validated React flight payload.
Use the same execution primitive to inventory `.next/server`, action manifests, and environment markers before escalating further.

preferred_order: First prove the RSC surface with a harmless GET probe that returns text/x-component.
Then confirm Server Action parsing with a benign next-action probe instead of jumping straight to command execution.
Once the route and action path are confirmed, substitute the smallest possible `{cmd}` value and collect version / runtime context before any heavier follow-up.
shell_prerequisites: Confirm the selected route is served by the App Router and not rewritten to a Pages Router-only path.
Confirm Origin / Host handling, reverse proxies, and middleware do not reject handcrafted Server Action requests before they reach Next.js.
Prefer targets where the response behavior clearly proves RSC or Server Action parsing, even if the first probe only yields an error frame.
fallbacks: If one route does not expose RSC behavior, probe other App Router routes or route groups before ruling the target out.
If invalid next-action probes are normalized away by middleware, fall back to GET-based RSC surface confirmation and version fingerprinting.
If the deployment sits behind a temporary provider mitigation, treat it as a blocker for exploitation but not as evidence that the underlying version is patched.

detection_notes: Official advisories warn that hosting-provider WAF rules were only temporary mitigations and should not be treated as a substitute for patching.
Useful log and telemetry markers include _rsc, rsc, next-action, text/x-component, and x-nextjs-action-not-found.
Malformed probes may still return 404 or 500 while proving the request reached Next.js's RSC / Server Action parser.
references: React blog: Critical Security Vulnerability in React Server Components (CVE-2025-55182)
